title: CPE C2 Proficiency Reading & Use of English - Test 1 (Cambridge CPE 3)
source: Cambridge Certificate of Proficiency in English 3, Cambridge University Press

sections.title: Part 1: Multiple-Choice Cloze
sections.instructions: For questions 1-18, read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap.
sections.passageHtml:
Sharing a Room

Before starting university, I hadn't given more than a passing thought to what could go wrong sharing a room with another student. I had simply assumed that my roommate-to-be would be rather like me — at university to have fun but mainly to learn. Within a couple of weeks, I was (1) ______ aware of how wrong I was. The (2) ______ proved to be the complete opposite of the studious type I had envisaged. It didn't take long before things came to a (3) ______ and we had our first major row about, of all things, the alarm clock going off every day at 7:00 am. The next day, I (4) ______ up the courage to have a quiet word with our student liaison officer, who explained that sharing a room with someone is about learning to compromise. I had to (5) ______ up the pros and cons and try to come to some sort of arrangement. Compromise soon proved to be the key as my roommate's initial hostility was slowly (6) ______ down once I managed to persuade him to agree to a workable set of ground rules. From then on it was relatively smooth sailing, though I still had to keep a (7) ______ grip on my temper. Two semesters later, we (8) ______ up the best of friends.

sections.title: Part 2: Open Cloze
sections.instructions: For questions 1-15, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each space. Use only one word in each space.
sections.passageHtml:
Fake Rolex Watches

Rolex is ONE of the most sought (9) ______ brand names in the timepiece industry. (10) ______ from the design and movement of a Rolex to its price screams quality. Thanks to the outrageous prestige of the Rolex brand, Rolex watches are (11) ______ the most counterfeited watches in the world. The Rolex brand carries (12) ______ it a sense of status and luxury that is recognised worldwide. It (13) ______ without saying that thrift motivates informed fake Rolex purchases. Most people simply don't have the money to purchase the genuine article. There is only one way to tell a high-quality Rolex knock-off (14) ______ the real deal and that is to get an expert to open up the watch. At this point, most experts, most of the time, can spot a fake by looking at the watch's movement. But not always. Sometimes, even experts can have a hard time telling the difference, especially (15) ______ they are looking at a high-quality fake. And Rolex replicas are only going to get better. This means they are going to become (16) ______ harder to detect.

questionGroups.instruction: Write the correct word in each gap in CAPITAL LETTERS.

sections.title: Part 3: Word Formation
sections.instructions: For questions 16-25, read the text below. Use the word given in capitals at the end of some of the lines to form a word that fits in the space in the same line.
sections.passageHtml:
Employee Sick Leave

The European Court of Justice has ruled that workers who become ill during their holidays have the right to take ADDITIONAL paid time off later. The Luxembourg-based court stated that 'the purpose of (17) ______ (TITLE) to paid annual leave is to enable the worker to rest and enjoy a period of (18) ______ (RELAX) and leisure'. It said this right applies even if a worker becomes unwell while on leave, even if it is due to a pre-existing (19) ______ (AIL). Business groups in the UK say that the courts' rules are (20) ______ (CONTRADICT). Business groups in the UK warn that applying the courts' rules will be (21) ______ (DETRIMENT) to the wider UK economy and say that the rulings (22) ______ (LINE) the need for Britain's ministers to resist the court. But, unfortunately, while the UK has an opt-out from the EU's working time (23) ______ (DIRECT), it does not extend to annual leave. The UK government estimates that adopting the rules will cost British employers more than £100 million annually. The UK's Business Secretary described the court's rulings as 'an unnecessary burden on businesses' and 'completely (24) ______ (JUSTIFY) during a time of economic strain'.

questions.instruction: The manufacturing process was delayed by problems no one had foreseen. (held) -> The manufacturing process ____________________ problems.
acceptedAnswers: was held up because of unforeseen
was held up due to unforeseen
was held up owing to unforeseen
was held up on account of unforeseen
was held up as a result of unforeseen
was held up because of unexpected
was held up due to unexpected
was held up owing to unexpected
was held up on account of unexpected
was held up as a result of unexpected
questions.explanation: Đáp án: WAS HELD UP BY UNFORESEEN. Giải thích: Cụm động từ 'hold up' nghĩa là trì hoãn (delay). 'Problems no one had foreseen' tương đương với 'unforeseen problems' (vấn đề chưa được lường trước) hoặc 'unexpected problems'. Do đó, cấu trúc bị động được dùng là 'was held up by/due to/because of unforeseen/unexpected problems'. Chuyên đề: Phrasal verbs - Hold up & Passive voice & Word formation.

questions.correctAnswer: without her brother advising her when
questions.explanation: Đáp án: WITHOUT HER BROTHER ADVISING HER WHEN (hoặc các biến thể danh động từ như without her brother's advising/having advised). Giải thích: 'Without someone doing something/having done something' = Nếu không có ai đó làm việc gì. Do đó, 'if her brother hadn't advised her' viết lại thành 'without her brother advising/having advised her'. Chuyên đề: Gerund constructions & Conditionals. Meo/Trap: xac dinh loai gap truoc khi chon: word form, collocation, dependent preposition, clause pattern hoac idiom. Kiem tra tu ngay truoc/sau gap, so it/so nhieu, tense/aspect va register. Bay thuong gap la dap an dung nghia nhung sai cau truc hoac sai collocation.

Giải thích: Đầu câu bắt đầu bằng trạng từ phủ định 'Very rarely', do đó ta cần áp dụng cấu trúc đảo ngữ (Inversion): 'Rarely/Seldom + auxiliary verb + S + V'. Ở đây dùng trợ động từ 'does' cho chủ ngữ 'Tim', kết hợp cụm 'get the opportunity/chance to do something' (có cơ hội làm gì). Chuyên đề: Inversion (Đảo ngữ) & Collocations.

sections.title: Part 5: Multiple Choice
sections.instructions: For questions 19-40, read the texts below and choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the texts.
sections.passageHtml:
Emma's Visit

Emma Boylan drove at her usual breakneck speed through the city traffic, manoeuvring with consummate skill to get past the slower-moving vehicles. Her green TR7 attracted a lot of attention, most of it hostile. Apart from Vinny, few people had seen the car before she bought it. Vinny had inspected it and expressed qualms. Was it not a bit too powerful for a woman driver, he offered. That clinched it. She bought the car the next day.

Today she had decided to call on her father unannounced at his practice. Her father did not encourage the habit, nor was it something she particularly enjoyed herself. Twice a year she felt the need to present herself at his offices. It helped maintain an illusion of father-daughter togetherness that she felt was expected of them.

She was shown into his office by one of the junior solicitors, a young man so thin and pale that he looked as though a breath of fresh air might blow him away. Her father's office, lined with heavy bookshelves and hung with photographs of himself and his family at various stages of their lives, struck her as oppressive. She could not envisage working in such an environment. Emma suspected that the display was more for the benefit of visitors than for the man sitting behind the desk.

Her father, a tall broad-shouldered man whose face bore traces of what must once have been considerable good looks, waved her into a chair good-naturedly. 'You've been out gallivanting again,' he said, doing a passable impression of a barrister addressing the court. 'What have you been up to? I know you never do anything without a reason. So, why are you here? What do you want?'

'Can't a girl visit her own father without there having to be a reason?' she responded, sounding suitably indignant. 'Oh, come now, dear girl,' he said with a wave of the hand, 'we both know the answer to that one.'

sections.passageHtml:
Playing the Game

The UK games industry is now worth over £1 billion and is one of the most vibrant sectors of the creative economy. It employs thousands of highly skilled people in roles ranging from programming and art to marketing and quality assurance. Yet for all its commercial success, the industry remains surprisingly poorly understood by those seeking to enter it.

[Gap 37]

Today the industry is very commercial, very competitive and very much technology-driven. The cost of developing a top title for a next-generation console can easily exceed £5 million, and the financial risk involved means that publishers are increasingly reluctant to take chances on unproven talent.

[Gap 38]

Nick Wheelwright, head of development at a major UK studio, confirms this trend. 'We are looking for outstanding people with excellent academic records,' he says. 'The days when you could walk in off the street with a demo tape and land a job are well and truly over.'

[Gap 39]

The skills required extend well beyond pure programming. Publishers need people with expertise in marketing, business development and project management. A flair for communication and the ability to work under pressure are highly prized.

[Gap 40]

At the other end of the scale, games testers represent the most accessible entry point into the industry. Testing is often dismissed as unskilled work, but experienced testers develop an intimate knowledge of game mechanics and software behaviour that is genuinely valuable.

[Gap 41]

Rachel Wood is a senior lead artist at one of the UK's most respected studios. Her path into the industry was far from conventional and illustrates the range of backgrounds from which successful game developers can emerge.

[Gap 42]

Rachel emphasises the importance of understanding technology even in a creative role. 'You can be the most talented artist in the world,' she says, 'but if you can't work within the technical constraints of the hardware, your work will never make it into a game.'

[Gap 43]

Whatever route aspiring game developers choose to take, the message from the industry is clear: passion for games is necessary but not sufficient. Technical competence, creative flair and professional discipline are all essential ingredients for a successful career in this demanding but rewarding field.

questionGroups.instruction: Match the correct paragraph (A-H) to each gap.

paragraphOptions.label: A
paragraphOptions.text: And even if the two of us failed to challenge the top musical composers successfully, then we could try to knock the Beatles and Rolling Stones off their perches later, in the summer. The Everly Brothers had just made a comeback and would clearly be in need of some new material.

paragraphOptions.label: B
paragraphOptions.text: By then I would have surely grown out of pop music as my father had confidently predicted I would by the time I was twenty-one. This was worrying - if he was right I only had a few more months of enjoying it.

paragraphOptions.label: C
paragraphOptions.text: As he confidently continued to bash out selections from some of the many shows he had written and produced at school, I was reminded of many of the best show albums from my parents' LP collection. He needed a new lyricist for the outside world.

paragraphOptions.label: D
paragraphOptions.text: But it seemed to me that plenty of other blokes around my age (twenty) and not overburdened with talent were making it and I wished to be of their number. There were even guys from my home town and from public school in the pop charts - surely I had the qualifications.

paragraphOptions.label: E
paragraphOptions.text: I wasn't convinced by the idea for a musical that he had been working on for the past year, but in 1965 I was rarely convinced about anything. His talent was beyond question and he claimed to have all the contacts. I was soon back in his drawing room.

paragraphOptions.label: F
paragraphOptions.text: My own ambitions were just as insane as his; I wanted to be a pop star, for all the healthy reasons - women, money and fame. The difference between Andrew and myself was that my dreams were never life or death to me, though it's easy to say that now. They might have become so had I failed.

paragraphOptions.label: G
paragraphOptions.text: On the other hand, Andrew's conviction of his score's precocious brilliance was infectious and not totally unjustified. What did I know about musicals? As David Land, later to be my agent for over a quarter of a century, memorably (and repeatedly) said, if there's a demand for one hamburger bar on the block, there is room for two. We could be the second hamburger joint.

paragraphOptions.label: H
paragraphOptions.text: Here was the largest collection of records I had ever seen, the first stereo record player and tuner I had come across and the astonishing evidence that a teenager existed who had spent money on Georgian wine glasses, pictures and furniture.
